tool_tagline: The answer engine that solved Google's link overload -- and created a new problem
overview: Perplexity sits at a unique position in the AI landscape - not fully a chatbot, not a full-fledged search engine. It is more of an answer engine, which can synthesize and process real-time web content into cited conversational responses. In a way, it solves for the problems of Google Search's link overload and ChatGPT's hallucination in a go. Google Search generates links of all pages which can even remotely hold the answers to your questions. Perplexity takes that information and generates conversational responses out of it, not overwhelming users with bunch of pages. Since it cites all its responses, it tries to solve for the hallucination problem also.

primary.name: Students & Academics
primary.description: Citation based answers, academic filters, access to peer-reviewed sources.

primary.name: Researchers & Analysts
primary.description: Real-time synthesis of current data with verifiable sources. Deep Research and Spaces features create persistent knowledge environments.

primary.name: Journalists & Fact Checkers
primary.description: Real-time source verification helps with rapid background research of developing stories.

secondary.name: Content Writers
secondary.description: Strong for research & ideation, falls short for long form generation and creative output.

secondary.name: Developers & Engineers
secondary.description: Useful for quick technical lookups, but not a coding companion.

jtbd: When I need to do deep research with cited, verifiable sources
When I need to fact-check a claim quickly and trace it to a primary source
When I am writing an academic paper and need sourced references
When I need market and competitor research with up-to-date information

what_works.title: Citation first transparency
what_works.description: Perplexity's foundational differentiator by design. Helps build trust, critical for any kind of research, academic, or fact checking.

what_works.title: Source filtering
what_works.description: Users have the ability to filter which source they want the results from - social, academic, or web.

what_works.title: Deep Research & Spaces
what_works.description: Provides pro users with organized research environments. Users can create an AI "expert" in a specific domain by uploading documents and setting context.

what_works.title: Multi-model flexibility
what_works.description: Perplexity enables users to choose between different models for different tasks - all within a single interface.

what_doesnt_work.title: Billing and Plan Failures with Zero Human Support
what_doesnt_work.description: I am surprised to find out something this basic is a #1 problem for an otherwise complex product. Users have complained about limits downgrading mid-subscription, double-charging, or subscriptions being cancelled without any refunds or reasonings. Raising complaints results in auto-generated responses with no clear justification or path forward.

what_doesnt_work.title: Hallucination with attribution problem
what_doesnt_work.description: What was designed as a way to manage hallucination problem ended up creating a deeper problem. Perplexity does cite sources of the responses, but the citations have been known to be identified as incorrect. This is an even bigger problem because it makes the users believe that the responses are sourced and cited, but it is hallucinating with authority.

what_doesnt_work.title: Reproduction of licensed content
what_doesnt_work.description: By early 2026, Perplexity is facing lawsuits from various news corps on the ground that it is performing unauthorized scraping and reproduction of copyrighted content without licensing it. Its entire business model is under review at the moment. This is not just a legal trouble for Perplexity, but for the users also who rely on such content for academic and legal research.

what_doesnt_work.title: Stateless sessions
what_doesnt_work.description: It treats every query as stateless and often forgets the context of previous queries. Research workflows require building on prior context across sessions, this is a genuine limitation.

recommendation.title: Build for trust -- at every layer
recommendation.summary: Perplexity's core value proposition is trust: cited answers, verifiable sources, no ads. But trust is being eroded at multiple layers simultaneously. The product opportunity is not a new feature -- it is fixing the existing ones.

opportunities.title: FIX CUSTOMER SERVICE!
opportunities.description: Rebuild the process ground up. Align the billing plans with the policy and announce transparently; whenever policy is updated mid-subscription, send informational emails to the customers beforehand and give them an option to opt-out with refund of the balance payment; build a team of human agents to handle disputes, issues, and concerns. For a company whose foundational promise is trust (cited responses), this is too basic a miss! No fancy tech can replace honesty and dignity in service!

opportunities.title: Confidence scoring in Attribution
opportunities.description: While hallucination is an industry wide problem, we can still build for trust. At the end of every response, add whether the answer is directly sourced or is interpreted from multiple sources. If it is interpreted, users will know to double-check the source to verify the correctness. Users should not be in the blind; let them know when they are reading a conclusion, not a fact.

opportunities.title: I don't know as a feature
opportunities.description: Train the model to say sources don't clearly answer this rather than synthesizing a response when confidence is low. This is actually much more feasible for Perplexity to do than for pure LLMs, given its search-first architecture.

opportunities.title: Persistent memory for research workflows
opportunities.description: A research memory feature where Perplexity can retain context of the previous conversations within a space would dramatically increase the stickiness of the users who run long sessions. This will evolve Perplexity from an advanced search tool to a true research partner!

items: Number of billing-related support tickets per 1,000 subscribers per month
Average time to resolution for billing disputes (target: under 48 hours)
% of disputes resolved by human agent vs. auto-response
Escalation rate -- % of bot-handled tickets escalated to human agents
Refund approval rate and average processing time
Subscription churn rate, specifically tracking churn reason codes (billing vs. product vs. competition)
Click-through rate on cited sources, segmented by directly sourced vs. interpreted labels
% of responses flagged as interpreted synthesis vs. directly sourced -- tracks model honesty over time
User correction rate -- how often users flag a cited response as incorrect
I don't know response rate, paired with a satisfaction score on those responses
NPS segmented by tier: free vs. Pro vs. Enterprise
Session depth on research tasks -- are users completing multi-step research flows or dropping off?

komals_rating.label: Komal's Rating
komals_rating.reasoning: Perplexity earns a strong score for what it set out to do -- real-time cited answers in a clean, ad-free interface. For researchers, students, and fact-checkers, it is genuinely the best tool in its category. The score is held back by a billing and support operation that contradicts its trust-first brand, a hallucination problem that is arguably more dangerous than its competitors' because it looks authoritative, and an existential legal question about its content model. The bones are excellent. The operational execution is not.

pm_take: Perplexity is one of the most interesting PM case studies in AI right now -- not because of what it built, but because of the contradiction it built into itself. The entire product is a promise of trust. Cited answers. Verifiable sources. No ads. Skip the links, get the truth. And yet the #1 complaint from paying users is that they were double-charged and a chatbot ignored them for four days. You cannot sell trust as your product and have the worst billing support in the category. That is not a gap -- it is a brand contradiction at the foundation. The hallucination problem is equally revealing. Perplexity did not invent hallucination -- every LLM hallucinates. But by attaching citations to every response, Perplexity made hallucinations look authoritative. A fabricated fact with a publisher's logo next to it is more dangerous than a fabricated fact with no attribution at all. The solution was not just to add citations -- it was to add confidence signals. Tell users when they are reading a synthesized conclusion, not a sourced fact. That distinction exists in the architecture. It just was not surfaced to the user. No fancy tech can replace honesty and dignity in service.
